summary: Jeff Atwood announces a major update to his CodeProject article about converting URLs to MHTML archive files using native .NET code. The MHTML format, based on RFC 2557, allows packaging entire web pages as single files that can be emailed or stored. The rewrite adds significant functionality including content encoding autodetection, HTTP compression support, frame handling, and authentication features. He positions this as useful for offline functionality in ASP.NET websites.
key_points: MHTML format packages entire web pages as single functional files based on RFC 2557
The rewrite adds international encoding detection, HTTP compression handling, and frame support
The solution enables quick offline functionality for ASP.NET sites via loopback HTTP requests
New features include HTTP authentication, proxy support, and configurable browser ID strings
money_quotes: It's basically a way to package an entire web page as a (mostly) functional single file that can be emailed, stored in a database, or what have you.
You may also know it as that crazy File, Save As, 'Web Archive, Single File' menu option in Internet Explorer.
This was a truly painful total rewrite, but it offers tons of new functionality.
key_insight: MHTML archives provide a practical solution for capturing and distributing complete web pages as self-contained files, with applications ranging from offline browsing to database storage.
